ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic pizza class (FOODON_00002475). Pizza Lecce is a specific restaurant variant with bacon, sun-dried tomatoes, egg, and ricotta; no exact FoodOn term exists for this combination.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers pizza as a category. Pizza Lecce with its specific toppings (bacon, sun-dried tomatoes, egg, ricotta) is not individually coded; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226789007 is the generic pizza concept. No specific code exists for Pizza Lecce variant.

serving.natural_language_portion: One whole Pizza Lecce as typically served in a restaurant, estimated at approximately 400 g, comprising the pizza base, sauce, mozzarella, bacon, sun-dried tomatoes, one egg, and dollops of ricotta cheese.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Pizza with meat, NS as to type of crust (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite); supplemented with USDA entries for ricotta cheese, bacon, sun-dried tomatoes, and egg for topping adjustment
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA entry exists for Pizza Lecce. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry for 'Pizza, cheese, regular crust' (FDC ID 170919) as the base, with upward adjustment to protein, fat, and saturated fat to reflect the addition of bacon, egg, and ricotta cheese toppings. Sun-dried tomatoes contribute minor additional sodium and sugar. The composite estimate is consistent with a loaded Italian-style pizza. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors (4/9/4 kcal per g protein/fat/carbohydrate).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; pizza entries including 'Pizza, Super Supreme, pan (Pizza Hut)' GI=36 and 'Pizza, Super Supreme, thin and crispy (Pizza Hut)' GI=30; general Italian-style pizza with regular crust estimated at GI 60 per University of Sydney GI Database composite data
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists specifically for Pizza Lecce. The University of Sydney GI Database lists multiple pizza variants ranging from GI 30 (thin crust, minimal toppings) to GI 80 (high-sugar variants). A regular-crust Italian-style pizza with protein-rich toppings (bacon, egg, ricotta) is estimated at GI 60, consistent with the medium-GI range reported for regular-crust pizzas in Atkinson et al. (2008), Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283. The protein and fat content from ricotta and bacon moderately blunt the glycemic response relative to a plain cheese pizza. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (60 × 110) / 100 = 66.0, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 27.5 × 4 = 110 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~560 mg per 100 g; ~2240 mg per whole pizza serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Pizza Lecce to occasional consumption, as a single serving may provide approximately 97–100% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg, driven primarily by bacon, mozzarella, and tomato sauce; portion reduction and requesting lower-sodium preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 60) and high glycemic load per serving (GL ~66)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be cautious with Pizza Lecce due to its substantial carbohydrate load (~110 g per whole pizza) and medium GI; consuming a smaller portion (one or two slices) alongside a salad can help moderate the postprandial glucose response, and the protein from ricotta, egg, and bacon provides some glycemic buffering.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from bacon, mozzarella, and ricotta (~4.6 g per 100 g; ~18.4 g per serving) and high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, Pizza Lecce should be consumed sparingly; the combination of saturated fat from multiple dairy and cured-meat sources and high sodium content is unfavourable for lipid profiles and blood pressure management, and portion control to one or two slices is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pizza Lecce is a loaded Italian-style pizza originating from the Apulian city of Lecce in southern Italy, served at Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore in Singapore. It is built on a classic Margherita base — a hand-stretched wheat dough topped with tomato sauce and melted mozzarella — and enriched with additional toppings: crispy bacon strips, intensely flavoured sun-dried tomatoes, a whole baked egg cracked directly onto the pizza during the final minutes of oven cooking, and generous dollops of fresh, creamy ricotta cheese added after baking. The result is a pizza that balances the tangy acidity of sun-dried tomatoes with the richness of ricotta and the savoury saltiness of bacon, while the egg adds a silky, custardy element. Nutritionally, Pizza Lecce is a calorie-dense dish (~252 kcal per 100 g) with a meaningful protein contribution (~12.5 g per 100 g) from the egg, ricotta, bacon, and mozzarella. It is high in sodium (~560 mg per 100 g) due to the cured bacon and cheese, and contains moderate saturated fat (~4.6 g per 100 g). The glycemic index is estimated at 60 (medium), and a whole pizza serving carries a high glycemic load (~66). Clinically, it is a concern for hypertension due to sodium, and requires portion awareness for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease. It contains gluten, dairy, and eggs, and is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those avoiding pork.
common_user_queries: What toppings are on Pizza Lecce?
How many calories in Pizza Lecce?
Is Pizza Lecce suitable for diabetics?
What is the difference between Pizza Lecce and Margherita pizza?
Where can I get Pizza Lecce in Singapore?

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry exists for Pizza Lecce in any approved primary source. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy data for cheese pizza with regular crust, with compositional adjustments for bacon, egg, and ricotta toppings. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database pizza data. Confidence is medium rather than low because the adaptation methodology is transparent, the ingredient list is well-defined, and the USDA source is a high-quality approved database.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates based on USDA SR Legacy 'Pizza, cheese, regular crust' (FDC ID 170919) as the base, with upward adjustments to protein, fat, saturated fat, cholesterol, sodium, calcium, and vitamin B12 to reflect the addition of bacon (~15 g per 100 g pizza), one whole egg (~10 g per 100 g pizza), and ricotta cheese (~12 g per 100 g pizza). 2. Serving size of 400 g is an estimate for a standard restaurant whole pizza; actual weight may vary. 3. GI of 60 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Pizza Lecce exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA composite data for the ingredient mix; these are not directly measured values. 5. This is an Italian cuisine dish; multilingual naming rules for Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, and Vietnamese dishes do not apply. No mandatory non-English local names are required. 6. The dish is not halal due to the presence of bacon (pork). 7. Price data (SGD 32.59) is preserved from the field survey source record for reference only and is not used as a nutritional evidence source.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full RESULT JSON from input seed record. Adapted USDA SR Legacy pizza nutrient data for the specific topping composition (bacon, egg, ricotta, sun-dried tomatoes). Estimated GI at 60 from University of Sydney GI Database analogous pizza data. Populated all three health context conditions with specific clinical guidance. Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases are perfectly consistent. Confirmed Italian cuisine does not require mandatory non-English local names. Verified GI 60 maps to gi_category 'medium' (56-69 threshold). Verified all per_100g values are numeric with no zeros in core macros. Verified all -1 sentinel values are documented in notes. Verified ontology match_type values use only allowed strings (closest_parent). Self-scored at 8/8 with all hard gates passing.

Iteration 2: Reviewed and confirmed consistency of aliases_by_language vs flat aliases. Confirmed zero-macro justification rule not triggered (no core macro is zero). Confirmed sentinel value documentation rule satisfied in notes. Confirmed GI category threshold rule: GI 60 is correctly classified as medium. Final decision confirmed as PASS.
